55240.meaning_1: The discourse on volition regarding forms
55240.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The lemma is a compound sutta name. The contextual sentence lists it in the Saṃyutta Nikāya (SN25.7), within the Okkantasaṃyutta. It is a kammadhāraya compound where "rūpasañcetanā" (volition regarding forms) modifies "sutta" (discourse). The meaning is derived from this context.

55187.meaning_1: Discourse on Craving for Forms; Sutta about Craving for Visible Objects
55187.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is the eighth sutta in the Okkantasaṃyutta (chapter 25) of the Saṃyutta Nikāya. The title is a descriptive compound referring to a discourse (sutta) whose subject is craving (taṇhā) for form (rūpa). The meaning_1 is derived from its placement in the Nikāya and standard translations of such titles. The compound analysis is a genitive tappurisa (rūpe taṇhā, "craving for form"), which is then compounded with "sutta".

60522.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
Headword is a sutta title. The lemma_1 field should not have the trailing number 1; sutta titles in related examples (e.g., akaṭa 1) use the number to distinguish homographs, but here "samphassajasutta" is a unique sutta name and should be lemma_1: samphassajasutta. The contextual sentence shows it is the fifth sutta of the "uppādasaṃyuttaṃ". The compound is a kammadhāraya (determinative) compound: "samphassaja" (born from contact) + "sutta" (discourse). The meaning should reflect this.

69613.meaning_lit: discourse on that which is born of seclusion
69613.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
Sutta title. The compound describes the first jhāna, which is born from (ja) seclusion (viveka) from sensuality. Context shows it is the first sutta in the Sāriputtasaṃyutta.

10582.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This sutta is from the Saṃyutta Nikāya, likely discussing the meditative state without thought (avitakka), associated with the second jhāna.

46411.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is the third sutta (pītisuttaṃ 3) in the Sāriputtasaṃyutta (SN28.3). The meaning 'discourse on joy' is directly indicated by the compound components: pīti (joy, rapture) and sutta (discourse). This is a standard naming pattern for suttas, describing their primary subject matter.

16473.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is the fourth sutta in the Sāriputtasaṃyutta of the Saṃyutta Nikāya. It deals with the fourth jhāna, characterized by equanimity (upekkhā). The lemma_1 was corrected to remove the extraneous "1", as it is a distinct text title.

11073.meaning_1: The Discourse on the Base of Infinite Space
11073.meaning_lit: discourse on the base of infinite space
11073.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is a formal title of a specific sutta within the Saṃyutta Nikāya. The literal meaning "discourse on the base of infinite space" reflects the sutta's content, which deals with the first immaterial attainment (ārūpajjhāna). The construction is ākāsa (space) + ananta (infinite, boundless) + āyatana (base, sphere) + sutta (discourse). In Pāḷi, "ananta" often appears in its neuter singular locative form "anante" or combined forms like "ānañca" in compounds.

67714.meaning_1: discourse on the base of infinite consciousness
67714.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is a sutta of the Saṃyutta Nikāya (SN 28.6), specifically the sixth sutta in the Sāriputtasaṃyutta. The title is a compound of "viññāṇañcāyatana" (the base of infinite consciousness, a jhānic attainment) and "sutta" (discourse).

11093.meaning_1: Sutta about the Dimension of Nothingness
11093.meaning_lit: discourse on the base/plane of nothingness
11093.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is the seventh sutta in the Sāriputtasaṃyutta (SN 28.7). It is a compound where 'ākiñcaññāyatana' (the sphere/plane of nothingness) is modified by 'sutta' (discourse). The sutta discusses the attainment of the sphere of nothingness and is part of a series on the formless attainments.

38852.meaning_1: The eighth discourse in the Sāriputtasaṃyutta, concerning the attainment of the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception.
38852.meaning_lit: discourse on the dimension of neither perception nor non-perception
38852.family_compound: neva saññā na saññā āyatana sutta
38852.construction: neva + saññā + na + saññā + āyatana + sutta
38852.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This sutta title is a compound formed from "nevasaññānāsaññāyatana" (the base object of the discourse) and "sutta" (discourse). The first element itself is a complex kammadhāraya compound describing the eighth immaterial attainment (āyatana), which is the main topic of the discourse between Sāriputta and the Buddha.

38116.meaning_1: Discourse on the Attainment of Cessation
38116.meaning_lit: discourse on the attainment of cessation
38116.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
A compound title consisting of 'nirodha' (cessation, stopping), 'samāpatti' (attainment, accomplishment), and 'sutta' (discourse, thread). The compound is a tappurisa (determinative) where the first element qualifies the second: a sutta (discourse) concerning (or about) the samāpatti (attainment) of nirodha (cessation). The literal meaning is 'discourse on the attainment of cessation'. In the context provided (SN28.9, Sāriputtasaṃyutta), this sutta discusses the meditative attainment of cessation of perception and feeling (saññāvedayitanirodha).

64703.meaning_1: the discourse on the (river) with needle-like mouths
64703.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is the title of a sutta. The name is a compound. The first part, 'sūcimukhī' (lit. 'needle-mouthed'), is likely a proper noun describing a river or a location (a river with needle-like mouths, i.e., sharp, fast-flowing channels or a place of peril). As a sutta title, it is a neuter noun compound describing a specific teaching discourse from the Saṃyutta Nikāya.

15244.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence, uddhaṃmukha is contrasted with adhomukha, describing the posture of facing upwards while eating, as part of monastic conduct.
15244.example_1: na khvāhaṃ, bhagini, adhomukho bhuñjāmīti. tena hi, samaṇa, ubbhamukho [uddhaṃmukho] bhuñjasīti? na khvāhaṃ, bhagini, ubbhamukho bhuñjāmīti.

36870.meaning_2: a final, concluding, or explanatory statement
36870.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence, the word appears in a description of ascetics and brahmins who live by false livelihood. The phrase "nigamanavākye" seems to be part of a textual variant notation, as indicated by the square brackets. The term likely refers to a type of decisive or explanatory statement, perhaps used to clarify or summarize a point of doctrine or argument.
36870.example_1: kathañcarahi, samaṇa, bhuñjasīti? ye hi keci, bhagini, samaṇabrāhmaṇā [samaṇā vā brāhmaṇā vā nigamanavākye pana sabbatthāpi samāsoyeva dissati] vatthuvijjātiracchānavijjāya micchājīvena jīvikaṃ [jīvitaṃ] kappenti, ime vuccanti, bhagini, samaṇabrāhmaṇā adhomukhā bhuñjantīti. ye hi keci, bhagini, samaṇabrāhmaṇā nakkhattavijjātiracchānavijjāya micchājīvena jīvikaṃ kappenti, ime vuccanti, bhagini, samaṇabrāhmaṇā ubbhamukhā bhuñjantīti.

36067.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence from SN 29.1, "nāgayoniyo" is plural, meaning "dragon wombs" or "reproduction modes of dragons," referring to the four ways dragons are born as described in the sutta.
36067.example_1: “Mendicants, dragons reproduce in these four ways.

41573.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The headword is the name of the second sutta in the Nāgasaṃyutta (SN 29). From the contextual snippet "paṇītatarasuttaṃ", the meaning appears to refer to a sutta about the "more excellent" (paṇītara), likely comparing different types of nāgas or rebirths.

16519.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
Based on contextual sentence SN29.3, "nāgasaṃyuttaṃ, uposathasuttaṃ", meaning_1 is derived from the title. It is the 3rd sutta in the Nāgasaṃyutta, thus the sense is "Discourse on the Uposatha". The compound is a descriptive determinative (kammadhāraya) where uposatha (the fasting day) qualifies sutta (discourse).

70654.meaning_2: having transformed their bodies; having abandoned their bodies; with bodies left behind
70654.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the provided context, the compound describes nāgā (dragons) who, while keeping the uposatha, are in a transformed or altered state. The translation "having transformed their bodies" fits this context of shape-shifting or abandoning their usual draconic form. The Sanskrit is corrected from vyava + visṛṣṭa + kāya to vyavasṛṣṭa + kāya (vyava-√sṛj). The family_compound is updated from "vossaṭṭha kāya" to "vissaṭṭha kāya" to reflect the positive component (vissaṭṭha = abandoned) rather than the specific past participle form (vossaṭṭha).
70654.sutta_1: “ko nu kho, bhante, hetu, ko paccayo, yena m’idhekacce aṇḍajā nāgā uposathaṃ upavasanti vossaṭṭhakāyā ca bhavantī”ti?
70654.example_1: “Sir, what is the cause, what is the reason why some egg-born dragons keep the sabbath, having transformed their bodies?”

57098.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The entry is a sandhi of the conditional conjunction `sace` (if) and the temporal adverb `ajja` (today). In the contextual sentence (SN 29.3), it introduces a conditional clause specifying the present day as the time for performing good conduct, with the consequence of a favorable rebirth after death.
57098.sutta_1: sacajja mayaṃ kāyena sucaritaṃ careyyāma, vācāya sucaritaṃ careyyāma, manasā sucaritaṃ careyyāma, evaṃ mayaṃ kāyassa bhedā paraṃ maraṇā sugatiṃ saggaṃ lokaṃ upapajjeyyāma.
57098.example_1: If today we do good things by body, speech, and mind, when the body breaks up, after death, we may be reborn in a good place, a heavenly realm.

33184.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
Based on the contextual sentence from SN29.4, this is the title of the fourth sutta in the Nāgasaṃyutta, which is itself part of the Saṃyutta Nikāya. The meaning is derived directly from the component words: "dutiya" (second), "uposatha" (the observance day), and "sutta" (discourse). The construction is a descriptive (kammadhāraya) compound where "dutiya" qualifies "uposatha", and together they modify "sutta".

29445.meaning_lit: discourse on the third observance day
29445.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
Sutta title for the 5th discourse (SN29.5) in the Nāgasaṃyutta. The name is a compound meaning "the discourse concerning the third observance day". The contextual sentence is a citation from the Nidānasaṃyutta (likely a heading) naming the collection and the specific text.

25905.meaning_1: The Sutta on the Fourth Uposatha
25905.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The contextual citation is from the Nāgasaṃyutta (SN29), identifying this as the sixth sutta in that collection, specifically titled "Catuttha Uposatha Sutta". The compound is a straightforward determinative (kammadhāraya) describing a sutta that concerns the fourth observance day (uposatha).

33448.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is the name of a sutta (SN 29.8). The name likely refers to a discourse about a topic related to something "heard" (suta), being the second (dutiya) in a sequence or a second treatment of a theme. The provided contextual entry (SN29.8, nāgasaṃyuttaṃ, dutiyasutasuttaṃ) confirms this.

29508.meaning_1: the ninth discourse of the Nāga Saṃyutta; Sutta on the third heard or report
29508.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The word is a compound of the ordinal number "tatiya" (third), the past participle "suta" (heard, learned, a report), and "sutta" (discourse). The compound is a descriptive determinative (kammadhāraya) meaning "the third (or ninth, as it is the ninth sutta in the chapter) discourse concerning a report." Its contextual use is as a proper title for SN29.9.

25939.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The entry requires completion. The headword is a compound noun (sutta) composed of three elements: catuttha (fourth), suta (heard/learning), and sutta (discourse). The context is SN29.10 (Nāgasaṃyutta), confirming it is a specific discourse, the tenth in a series of four suttas on a topic related to hearing/learning, hence the meaning "The Fourth Sutra on Hearing". The literal meaning is "discourse on the fourth hearing". The pattern aligns with other sutta headwords: neuter, compound, kammadhāraya, stem ending in -a for neuter nouns. No root data is needed as it is a compound.

1989.meaning_1: the set of ten discourses on the benefit of giving to egg-born beings (nāgas)
1989.meaning_lit: discourse on the benefit of giving to the egg-born
1989.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is a sutta set from the Nāgasaṃyutta (SN29), specifically referring to ten discourses that discuss the merits and benefits of giving to nāgas, who are classified as egg-born beings in Buddhist cosmology.

28092.meaning_1: a set of thirty suttas beginning with the discourse on benefit and service regarding egg-born, etc.
28092.meaning_lit: discourse on benefit and service regarding egg-born, etc.
28092.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The contextual sentence identifies this headword as the title of a group of 30 suttas (suttas 21-50) within the Nāgasaṃyutta (SN 29). The compound is long and descriptive, breaking down as: jalābuja (egg-born, a type of nāga), ādi (etc./beginning with), dānūpakāra (benefit and service), sutta (discourse), tiṃsaka (group of thirty). It is a neuter noun because it refers to a collection of texts.

64064.meaning_1: mode of reproduction for a supernatural bird of prey
64064.meaning_2: mode of reproduction for a supernatural bird of prey; kind of birth for a garuḍa
64064.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence from SN 30.1, "supaṇṇayoniyo" (plural) is translated as "phoenixes reproduce... ways." The term refers specifically to the *type of womb* or *mode of birth* (yoni) pertaining to the class of mythical birds (supaṇṇa). Based on related compounds like "nāgayoni" (id 36067) and by analogy to the pattern of "yakkhayoni" (id 71596) and "tiracchānayoni" (id 30755), the compound type is a genitive tappurisa (chaṭṭhī), indicating the womb/origin belonging to or characteristic of a garuḍa. The Sanskrit cognate follows the sandhi of its components. The meaning_2 field expands slightly on the concept.
64064.example_1: “Mendicants, phoenixes reproduce in these four ways.

71015.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is a compound title consisting of the present participle "haranti" (taking) and "sutta" (discourse). The lemma_1 and lemma_2 are the same as it is a title and indeclinable in this context. The compound is a kammadhāraya (descriptive determinative). The contextual reference SN30.2 places it within the Supaṇṇasaṃyutta.

34303.meaning_1: Discourse on (one) who acts in a dual way
34303.meaning_lit: discourse on (one) who acts in a dual way
34303.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The contextual sentence identifies this as the third sutta (dvayakārīsuttaṃ) of the Supaṇṇasaṃyuttaṃ (SN 30). The meaning is derived from its compound parts: "dvaya" (dual), "kārī" (one who does/makes), and "sutta" (discourse). This is a neuter noun compound of the kammadhāraya (determinative) type.

33520.meaning_1: The group of three suttas, beginning with the second one, concerning the sutta on the pair of doers (Dvayakārīsutta).
33520.meaning_lit: discourse on the threefold division beginning with the second regarding the pair of doers
33520.family_compound: dutiyā ādi dvaya kārī sutta tattika
33520.construction: dutiyā + ādi + dvaya + kārī + sutta + tattika
33520.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is a technical term for a group of suttas (tattika = a set of three) in the Supaṇṇasaṃyutta. The meaning is derived from the provided context: the group comprises suttas SN30.3 (Dvayakārīsutta) and the following two, i.e., the "second, etc." (dutiyādi) set. The compound is formed from dutiyādi (second, etc.), dvayakārīsutta (the sutta on the pair of doers), and the taddhita suffix -tattika, forming a neuter noun.

28091.construction: jalābuja + ādi + dāna + upakāra + sutta + tiṃsaka
28091.compound_construction: jalābujādīnaṃ dānūpakāraṃ ārabbha suttaṃ (jalābujādisu tiṃsakaṃ suttaṃ)
28091.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
Based on the contextual reference "SN30.3, supannasaṃyuttaṃ, dvayakārīsuttaṃ, 17-46. jalābujādidānūpakārasuttatiṃsakaṃ", this is a compound term for a group of 30 suttas. The compound refers to "a set (tiṃsaka) of suttas concerning (upakāra?) the gift or service (dānūpakāra) related to egg-born beings, etc. (jalābujādi)". Jalābuja ("egg-born") is a type of being in Buddhist cosmology. Ādi means "and so on" or "et cetera". Dāna is "giving" or "gift". Upakāra is "help" or "service". Sutta is "discourse". Tiṃsaka is "a group of thirty". Thus the term likely denotes "The group of thirty discourses on the gift/service concerning the egg-born beings, etc." This is a naming convention for a subsection of the Supaṇṇasaṃyutta.

27283.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The contextual sentence provides the form "chacattālīsamaṃ", which is the neuter nominative/accusative singular of this ordinal number. The meaning "forty-sixth" is confirmed by its derivation from the cardinal number "chacattālīsa" (forty-six).

24316.meaning_1: belonging to the host of gandhabbas
24316.non_ia: belonging to the host of Gandhabbas (a class of deities)
24316.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence (sn31.1:1.3), the word is used adjectivally to qualify the masculine plural noun "deve" in the genitive case ("gandhabbakāyike ... deve"). It functions as a Bahubbīhi compound (possessive adjective) meaning "having a body/group (kāya) of Gandhabbas", i.e., belonging to that class of deities. The lemma provided in the original entry is correct as it is the vocative singular form used for adjectives. The grammar field has been corrected to include "comp" to indicate it is a compound.
24316.example_1: “Mendicants, I will teach you about the gods of the centaur realm.

47808.meaning_2: deities that inhabit fragrant sapwood; fragrant softwood
47808.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the provided contextual sentence (sn31.1:1.8), "pheggugandhe adhivatthā devā", the term is used in the locative plural ("pheggugandhesu") to indicate the class of deities residing in or inhabiting fragrant sapwood. The meaning "fragrant softwood" provided in the sentence fragment is a general translation of the compound. This is a standard Tappurisa compound meaning "the fragrance/gandha of the sapwood/pheggu".

42400.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence, the word is in the plural (papaṭikagandhe) and is qualified as the abode of deities (adhivatthā devā). The gloss given in the snippet, "fragrant shoots", suggests a specific, vegetal source for the fragrance, aligning with the "bark" meaning.

41866.meaning_1: fragrant leaves; devas residing in fragrant leaves
41866.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
From SN31.1: "santi, bhikkhave, pattagandhe adhivatthā devā." (There are, bhikkhus, devas dwelling in fragrant leaves.) The word refers to a class of deities whose abode is the fragrance of leaves. The compound is a tappurisa (genitive tatpurusa), meaning "fragrance of leaves."

47593.meaning_2: fragrance of fruit; the scent of fruit
47593.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence (santi, bhikkhave, phalagandhe adhivatthā devā), the term 'phalagandhe' is a locative plural, meaning 'in (those dwelling in) fragrant fruits' or 'in the fragrance of fruit.' The meaning 'fragrant fruit' is a tappurisa compound where 'gandha' (smell) belongs to 'phala' (fruit). Thus, devas are said to dwell in or preside over the scent of fruit.

54695.meaning_1: fragrance of sap; scent of juice
54695.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence SN 31.1, "rasagandhe" is in locative plural, meaning "in the fragrances of sap," where certain gods are said to dwell. The compound correctly interprets as a genitive tatpurisha (chaṭṭhī tappurisa), denoting the fragrance belonging to or derived from sap.

24294.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In sn31.1, gandhagandhe is locative plural, meaning "in fragrances," referring to the abode of certain deities.

63658.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is the name of the second sutta in the Gandhabbakāyasaṃyutta (SN 31). The title is a descriptive compound (kammadhāraya) meaning a "sutta concerning sucarita". The lemma_1 and lemma_2 are correctly given as sutta names. The meaning_1 is derived from the standard title "Discourse on Good Conduct".

53106.meaning_1: Discourse on the Root-Scent Giver; The Discourse on Giving Root Fragrances
53106.compound_construction: mūlagandhaṃ dātā yassa so mūlagandhadātā, taṃ suttaṃ
53106.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is the name of the third sutta in the Gandhabbakāyasaṃyutta (SN 31). The title is a compound describing a gandhabba (a celestial musician or tree spirit) who is a giver (dātā) of root fragrances (mūlagandha). The sutta likely discusses the kammic results of giving such fragrances.

53107.meaning_1: the group of ten discourses on (the plane of) root scents (and the) provision of gifts (i.e., the Sappasutta etc.)
53107.meaning_lit: discourse on (deeds pertaining to) root scents and gifts as help
53107.family_compound: mūla gandha dāna upakāra sutta dasaka
53107.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is a collective title (neuter singular) for a set of ten suttas (SN 31.13-22) from the Gandhabbakāyasaṃyutta. It is a compound enumerating the topics of the suttas: mūla- (root), gandha- (scent), dāna- (giving), upakāra- (assistance/benefit). The literal breakdown is "root-scent-gift-help-discourse-ten-group". The contextual sentence shows it as a heading within the Saṃyutta structure.

62438.meaning_1: the ninety discourses on the gift of assistance with essence, fragrance, etc.
62438.meaning_lit: discourse on the gift of assistance with essence, fragrance, etc., consisting of ninety
62438.family_compound: sāra gandha ādi dāna upakāra sutta navuti
62438.construction: sāra + gandha + ādi + dāna + upakāra + sutta + navutika
62438.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
Refers to suttas 23-112 in the Gandhabbakāyasaṃyutta, dealing with gifts and assistance related to essences and fragrances.

34337.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This ordinal number is a taddhita derivative of the compound cardinal number "dvādasasata" (112) using the suffix "-ima". The contextual citation is simply a list item, indicating its sequential, numerical function.

63104.meaning_1: chapter of ten discourses on the gift and help of cold clouds
63104.meaning_lit: discourse on gift and help of cold clouds group of ten
63104.sanskrit: śīta + balāhaka + dāna + upakāra + sūkta, sūtra + daśaka
63104.family_compound: sīta valāhaka dāna upakāra sutta dasaka
63104.construction: sīta + valāhaka + dāna + upakāra + sutta + dasaka
63104.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is a book/chapter title from the Saṃyutta Nikāya. The compound is a descriptive (kammadhāraya) name for a set (dasaka) of ten discourses (sutta) concerning the giving (dāna) and assistance (upakāra) related to cold (sīta) clouds (valāhaka). The contextual sentence shows its usage as a chapter heading.

14588.meaning_1: forty suttas on the gift and assistance of hot clouds
14588.meaning_lit: discourse on the gift and assistance of hot clouds
14588.sanskrit: uṣṇa + balāhaka + dāna + upakāra + sūkta, sūtra + ?
14588.family_compound: uṇha valāhaka dāna upakāra sutta cālīsaka
14588.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is the title of a collection of forty suttas (cālīsaka) within the Valāhakasaṃyutta, specifically concerning the gift (dāna) and service/assistance (upakāra) related to hot clouds (uṇha valāhaka). The contextual sentence shows it is the name of a subsection (13-52) of SN 32.

34478.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence from sn32.13-52, it refers to the fifty-second item in a series, likely in a numerical list.

63105.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is a discourse title from the Valāhakasaṃyuttaṃ (Connected Discourses on Cloud Devas), specifically dealing with the deities of cold clouds. The compound is a kammadhāraya (descriptive determinative) where 'sīta' (cold) describes the type of 'valāhaka' (cloud), and together they describe the 'sutta' (discourse).

31242.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence "sn32.53:1.8 tepaññāsamaṃ.", the term is used as an ordinal number (in the accusative singular neuter form) to enumerate a specific item, likely the fifty-third in a series, within the Sutta Nipāta.

14589.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
Based on the contextual reference (SN32.54, uṇhavalāhakasuttaṃ), this is a compound title of a sutta. The literal meaning of the compound components is "hot-cloud", referring to a type of cloud deity (deva). The meaning_1 and meaning_lit fields are left empty as per the original data structure, but the sutta title would typically be translated as "The Discourse on Hot-Cloud Devas".

25975.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence from SN 32.54, "catupaññāsamaṃ" is used in a list to denote the fifty-fourth item in a sequence, consistent with its role as an ordinal number. The suffix "-ma" is the standard taddhita suffix for forming ordinals from cardinal numbers.

7291.meaning_1: Discourse on clouds and rain clouds
7291.meaning_lit: discourse on clouds and rain clouds
7291.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This sutta is part of the Valāhakasaṃyutta (Connected Discourses on Cloud Devas) in the Saṃyutta Nikāya, discussing phenomena related to clouds and rain clouds.

39823.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The contextual sentence (sn32.55:1.8) uses the neuter singular form "pañcapaññāsamaṃ" to denote the 55th item in a sequence, confirming its ordinal function. The construction uses the cardinal base "pañcapaññāsa" (55) with the ordinal suffix "-ma".

66753.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This sutta is from the Valāhakasaṃyutta (SN 32), focusing on deities related to wind and clouds. The compound vātavalāhaka is interpreted as "wind and clouds," and the sutta likely discusses their nature or associated teachings. The Sanskrit includes sūkta, which may be a variant or error for sūtra, the standard equivalent for sutta.

27480.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
Used in Sn32.56 to denote the fifty-sixth item in a series or list.

55159.meaning_1: Sutta on (questions about) non-knowledge regarding form (materiality)
55159.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This is the first sutta of the Vacchagottasaṃyutta (SN 33.1), dealing with the wanderer Vacchagotta asking the Buddha about the reasons why various speculative views arise. The title refers to ignorance or non-knowledge (aññāṇa) regarding the aggregate of form (rūpa).

20562.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
The headword "karotosutta" is a proper noun, the name of a sutta. Based on the contextual sentence (SN24.6) and the provided family_set, it belongs to a group of suttas. The compound is a kammadhāraya (descriptive determinative) formed from "kato" (past participle of karoti, meaning "made", "done", "acted") and "sutta" ("discourse"). Therefore, the literal meaning is "discourse on (the theme of) being made/acted/done".

51731.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
Contextually, this is the eighth sutta of the Diṭṭhisaṃyutta (Connected Discourses on Views). The compound is a descriptive determinative (kammadhāraya) meaning "the discourse concerning/characterized by the great view(s)."

32748.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence, 'dīghamajjhimesu' (locative plural) refers to the Dīgha and Majjhima Nikāyas, indicating these collections in the list of teachings.
32748.example_1: sattime (bahūsu)] kāyā akaṭā, akaṭavidhā, animmitā, animmātā, vañjhā, kūṭaṭṭhā esikaṭṭhāyiṭṭhitā, te na iñjanti, na vipariṇamanti, na aññamaññaṃ byābādhenti, nālaṃ aññamaññassa sukhāya vā dukkhāya vā sukhadukkhāya vā. yopi tiṇhena satthena sīsaṃ chindati, na sopi kañci [na koci kañci, na koci taṃ] jīvitā voropeti, sattannaṃtveva kāyānamantarena satthaṃ vivaramanupavisati [vivaramanupatati (katthaci) dīghamajjhimesupi] . cuddasa kho panimāni yonipamukhasatasahassāni saṭṭhi ca satāni cha ca satāni pañca ca kammuno satāni pañca ca kammāni, tīṇi ca kammāni, kamme ca aḍḍhakamme ca dvaṭṭhipaṭipadā, dvaṭṭhantarakappā, chaḷābhijātiyo, aṭṭhapurisabhūmiyo, ekūnapaññāsa ājīvakasate, ekūnapaññāsa paribbājakasate, ekūnapaññāsa nāgavāsasate, vīse indriyasate, tiṃse nirayasate, chattiṃsarajodhātuyo, satta saññīgabbhā, satta asaññīgabbhā, satta nigaṇṭhigabbhā, satta devā, satta mānusā, satta pesācā, satta sarā, satta pavuṭā [sapuṭā, pavudhā], satta papātā, satta ca papātasatāni, satta supinā, satta supinasatāni, cullāsīti mahākappino [mahākappuno] satasahassāni, yāni bāle ca paṇḍite ca sandhāvitvā saṃsaritvā dukkhassantaṃ karissanti.

55328.meaning_1: Discourse on "The Self Has Form and Is Formless"; Sutta on "The Self is with Form and without Form"
55328.meaning_lit: discourse on the self with form and without form
55328.family_compound: rūpī ca arūpī ca attā sutta
55328.construction: rūpī ca arūpī ca attā + sutta
55328.compound_construction: rūpī ca arūpī ca attā + sutta
55328.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This sutta title from Diṭṭhisaṃyutta (SN 24) addresses the view "rūpī ca ahaṃ attā arūpī ca" ("The self is with form and without form"). The compound is a kammadhāraya (descriptive) combining the dvanda "rūpī ca arūpī ca" (with form and without form) with "attā" (self) and "sutta" (discourse). The headword in lemma_1 is a nominative singular neuter form, but lemma_2 is the more standard nominative singular neuter ending -aṃ.

42036.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This sutta is part of the Okkantasaṃyutta in the Saṃyutta Nikāya, discussing the earth element as a basis for contemplation or insight.

63796.meaning_1: the discourse on hearing; the heard discourse
63796.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This sutta is the seventh in the Nāgasaṃyutta of Saṃyutta Nikāya, and the title indicates it relates to teachings that are heard or learned, possibly in the context of serpent deities.

29238.meaning_1: fragrant bark; the scent of bark
29238.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
In the contextual sentence, it appears in the locative plural (tacagandhe) meaning "in (various types of) fragrant bark" or "among the fragrant-bark dwelling deities." The compound is a determinative compound (kammadhāraya), where "gandha" (scent) describes the type of "taca" (bark).

62437.meaning_1: the group of nine discourses on the givers of essence, perfume, etc.
62437.meaning_lit: discourse group on givers of essence, smell, and so forth
62437.family_compound: sāra gandha ādi dāta sutta navaka
62437.construction: sāra + gandha + ādi + dāta + sutta + navaka
62437.comment:
openrouter: deepseek/deepseek-v3.2
This refers to the ninth section (navaka) of suttas in the Gandhabbakāyasaṃyutta (SN 31), specifically dealing with those who give or are associated with essence, fragrance, and similar qualities.

66564.comment:

The headword is a title of a sutta. Based on the contextual references SN32.57 and valāhakasaṃyuttaṃ, this is the "Discourse on Rain-Clouds" within the Valāhaka Saṃyutta. The literal meaning is "rain-cloud discourse." The family_set value correctly identifies its location.
